html:
ProjectHoldings, TSE:9246

ProjectHoldings is not the construction value-engineering story in the older drafts. That is the wrong company. The real 9246 is a small Japanese DX services group: ProjectCompany does DX, AI, marketing and UI/UX consulting; artwize supplies engineers for development and testing; Dr. KENKO KEIEI provides industrial physician and health-management services. The point of owning it is not a hard-asset moat. It is a post-reset earnings recovery in a tiny, ignored, founder/SBI-backed TSE Growth company.

At the May 15, 2026 close of JPY 996, the equity value is roughly JPY 5.5 billion. Using Q1 cash of JPY 1.86 billion and borrowings of about JPY 1.09 billion, EV is roughly JPY 4.6 billion. The company is guiding FY2026 revenue of JPY 6.6 billion, operating profit of JPY 500 million, net income of JPY 340 million and EPS of JPY 63.90, so the stock is about 15.6x current-year earnings but only about 9x FY2026 EBIT and roughly 7x guided EBITDA. The setup gets much cheaper if management is even directionally right on the medium-term plan: JPY 8.0 billion revenue in FY2027, JPY 9.75 billion in FY2028, and EBITDA rising toward JPY 1.2 to 1.8 billion by FY2028, with a FY2030 EBITDA ambition of JPY 1.5 to 3.0 billion in the latest Q1 materials.

The market still prices the company like a busted 2021 growth IPO and messy consulting roll-up. That view is partly earned. 2023 to 2024 included overexpansion, high attrition, governance trouble from a former executive, expensive office costs, divestitures and a 2024 operating loss. But the variant perception is that those scars are the source of the mispricing, not the current state of the business. In FY2025, the company returned to profitability. In Q1 FY2026, sales hit a quarterly record of JPY 1.635 billion, up 19.3%, while ordinary profit rose 20% to JPY 86 million in the May 15 filing.

The important inflection is gross margin quality in the core DX business. The company has been reducing reliance on outside partners and using more internal consultants. In Q1, DX gross profit reached JPY 602 million, up 27.8% year over year, and the gross margin reached 50.0%. Consultant unit price improved 6.3% year over year to JPY 1.679 million per month. This is the operating leverage the market is missing. A consultant business that grows headcount but never lifts price is a body shop. A consultant business that grows headcount, cuts external subcontracting and raises unit price is a margin-recovery compounder.

Q1 did not look spectacular on headline operating margin, because hiring costs and seasonal factors hit early. That is exactly why the stock can still be cheap. Management says Q2 should be down from Q1 because seasonal utilization fades and new hires take time to become productive, while FY2026 profit is second-half weighted. If investors screen only Q1 operating profit progress, they see 17% of the full-year operating-profit target and move on. If they understand the cost timing, the better read is that revenue is already at 24.8% of guidance, the core gross margin is improving, and the company is investing into the higher-margin headcount base that supports FY2027 and FY2028 earnings.

The balance sheet also invalidates the stale bear case. This is not carrying a JPY 7 billion goodwill bomb. Q1 goodwill was only JPY 217 million, with customer-related intangibles of JPY 166 million. Equity ratio was 51.7%. Net cash is around JPY 770 million. The downside risk is not balance-sheet insolvency or impairment. It is execution: attrition, manager depth, recruiting cost, and whether unit prices hold.

Management is behaving like a company that wants the stock to work. On May 15 it announced a buyback of up to 30,000 shares, 0.56% of shares excluding treasury stock, for up to JPY 52.5 million, running from May 18 to August 12. The amount is small, but for a JPY 5.5 billion microcap it matters because it confirms the capital return framework is now active. The shareholder benefit program is also clearly designed for retail sponsorship, with annual benefit yields shown at up to 5.4% for 1,500 shares in the Q1 deck. The company is pushing retail IR through official X, note, Logmi events and shareholder-benefit-site briefings. That can look promotional, but in an illiquid Japanese microcap, retail ownership is part of the liquidity flywheel.

SBI is the strategic wild card. SBI Holdings owns roughly 29.9%, and the founder/CEO Yunosuke Doi remains a large holder, around 16% based on recent shareholder data. SBI’s stake is both validation and governance risk. It lowers float and can create minority-holder discomfort, but it also provides a strategic parent-like anchor, business referrals, credibility and potential exit optionality. For a JPY 5 billion company trying to sell DX and AI consulting into larger Japanese enterprises, that matters.

The business is not a monopoly, and it should not be valued like one. But it also should not trade like a broken staffing company if FY2027 EBIT can approach JPY 1 billion. At the current EV, that would be 4 to 5x forward EBIT. If FY2028 EBITDA lands even near the lower half of the JPY 1.2 to 1.8 billion range, today’s EV is only 2.5 to 4x that number. The real question is whether management’s medium-term plan is fantasy. Q1 does not prove it, but it supports the direction: record revenue, 50% DX gross margin, higher consultant pricing, growing headcount, expanding HR services, and a company now using buybacks and retail IR to pull the equity story forward.

The bear case is straightforward. Attrition has already stopped improving, rising to 18% in Q1, with 12 departures versus plan. Management says manager shortages contributed. If high-value consultants leave, recruiting costs stay elevated, unit prices stall, and the company misses the second-half profit ramp, the market will revert to “failed growth consultancy” mode. A stock that trades at 15x guided EPS can de-rate fast if the guide is cut.

The base case is that FY2026 guidance is achievable, FY2027 revenue approaches JPY 8 billion, and operating profit moves toward JPY 900 million to JPY 1.0 billion as internalization, pricing and SG&A normalization show up. A 12x to 13x earnings multiple on that earnings power, plus net cash, supports an equity value around JPY 12 billion.

The bull case is that ProjectHoldings is in the early part of a real margin rebuild. The DX business sustains 50%+ gross margin, artwize keeps recruiting engineers, Dr. KENKO KEIEI turns health services into a recurring cross-sell wedge, and FY2028 EBITDA reaches JPY 1.5 billion or better. In that case, a JPY 18 to 20 billion equity value is not aggressive for a small Japanese DX services company growing revenue near 20% with visible margin recovery.

Current stock price: JPY 996, May 15, 2026 close.

Bear target: JPY 650, down 35%. Guidance slips, attrition stays high, and the stock trades as a low-quality people business.

Base target: JPY 2,250, up 126%. FY2027 earnings power becomes visible and the market values the company at roughly JPY 12 billion.

Bull target: JPY 3,800, up 282%. FY2028 EBITDA approaches the company’s target range and the stock re-rates as a genuine DX margin-recovery compounder rather than a broken microcap.
